turns.content: I'm trying to set up live debugging for our embedded project, but the official docs on probe-rs feel overly abstract. Can you walk me through how to actually configure a development board so I can see variables in real time?

The core idea is that cargo-embed acts as your build driver and communicates directly with the probe-rs backend, which translates your debug symbols into hardware events. You start by editing your project's Embed.toml file to define the target chip and flash settings; this config tells probe-rs exactly where to write firmware and what debug protocol to use. Once cargo-embed compiles successfully, it generates an image that the probe can read, allowing you to attach a debugger and inspect memory without needing a separate boot loader or complex setup scripts.

Okay, so I get that cargo-embed handles the build output. But how do I actually trigger runtime logging from my firmware code itself? It seems like I'm missing something about how the data flows back to my IDE.

You integrate RTT (Rust Targeted Runtime) into your application by defining an RttLogWriter trait instance in your main function, which connects directly to the probe's serial port. When you call log functions from your code, they send formatted strings over the hardware interface instead of printing to a console that might be inaccessible during boot. This means your IDE receives live output streams as soon as the firmware runs, letting you watch variable states change instantly without stopping execution or waiting for logs to flush after a full cycle.
That makes sense now. So I don't need to manually parse log files anymore? My code will just automatically stream data to the debugger?

Exactly, once you've configured Embed.toml and linked RTT in your source, cargo-embed builds an image that probe-rs can flash and debug immediately. The combination of these tools means you get real-time visibility into your embedded state without manual intervention or external logging hardware; the compiler and debugger cooperate seamlessly to expose internal variables as they execute.

I have a working flash with cargo-embed but I'm getting stuck on the RTT logging setup. My current Embed.toml uses the default config which seems to just dump everything to UART without any filtering, so my debug output fills up the buffer instantly and blocks the flash cycle. Is there a better way to handle this?

You are right that the default configuration is too aggressive for production debugging. Instead of relying on the generic settings in Embed.toml, you should explicitly configure the probe-rs backend behavior by setting the rtt_port_num field directly. This allows you to allocate a specific UART port for RTT traffic without interfering with standard console output or flash operations. You can also enable the log_level trait in your crate configuration to filter out verbose messages that aren't relevant, which keeps the stream manageable.

That sounds good but I'm still confused about how probe-rs actually manages the flash memory mapping when cargo-embed is running. Does it automatically handle the vector table and reset vectors for me or do I need to manually configure those in my Rust code?

The cargo-embed tool handles the vector table and reset vector setup automatically through its internal linker script generation, so you don't need to manually configure those in your Rust code. However, probe-rs does require your binary to be linked with specific flags like --export-all-symbols to ensure it can correctly identify the start of memory for flashing operations. This is crucial because if the symbol table isn't complete, probe-rs might not know where the flash image actually begins.

Ah okay so I just need to add those linker flags in my Cargo.toml then? But wait, what happens if I run cargo-embed without probe-rs installed? Does it still work or do I absolutely need that ecosystem for debugging?

If you don't have probe-rs installed, cargo-embed will still successfully flash the firmware to your board using the generic STLink or JTAG interface supported by the tool. However, without probe-rs, you lose access to RTT logging and live debugging features entirely, so it becomes a simple one-shot flash operation only. The real value comes from integrating probe-rs which enables the interactive debugger session after the initial flash.
So my workflow is: build with specific flags, use cargo-embed to flash, then launch the probe-rs CLI to attach? And I should configure Embed.toml before running anything right?

Exactly. You must configure Embed.toml first to set up your target board and enable RTT logging settings like rtt_port_num. Then you run cargo-embed --release to flash the firmware into memory. Once that completes, you launch the probe-rs CLI tool which detects the connected hardware and automatically attaches to the running process for live debugging.
